assignment_description:
Color Theory: Expanded Color Wheel
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes prepared with Quinacridone Pink, Prussian Blue, and Hansa Yellow, brushes, water, cloth, printed color wheel

PREP: Print color wheel on white cardstock.
∞ Link: Color Wheel

➜ RECAP
Recall all you know about color thus far. Name the colors in the basic color wheel (three primary and three secondary colors). What do you recall about the expanded color wheel and tertiary colors?

➜ INTRO
It is important to review concepts at the beginning of the year. Today, we are going to make a new, expanded color wheel to refresh the skills we learned in level 4. We have two lesson periods to complete this, so there is no reason to rush.

➜ DISCUSS
Tertiary colors
• Tertiary colors are made by mixing a primary color with a secondary color.
• They are red-orange, yellow-orange, yellow-green, blue-green, blue-purple, and red-purple.
• Always state the primary and then the secondary color when naming the tertiary color.

➜ PRACTICE
Expanded color wheel (Complete over two lesson periods; work at your own pace.)
• Paint primary colors first; then secondary; then tertiary.
• Students will need to mix fresh secondary and tertiary colors.
• Refer to the example to see placement within the wheel.
• Color placement on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you make a mistake.
• The color wheel will be referred to throughout the term, so keep it in a handy place. One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your Art Book.
∞ Image Link: Example of Expanded Color Wheel
∞ Video Link: Create an Expanded Color Wheel L4 L49, L5 L1, & L6 L25

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Spend time looking outside and naming the colors that you see. Talk about the primary colors that you would use to mix those colors.

⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, color wheel, neutral pigment

➜ RECAP

➜ INTRO

➜ DISCUSS
Tertiary colors
• Tertiary colors are made by mixing a primary color with a secondary color.

• Always state the primary and then the secondary color when naming the tertiary color.

➜ PRACTICE
Complete expanded color wheel.
• Finish mixing colors and painting them in the appropriate places.
• Refer to the example if you are confused about placement within the wheel.
• Color placement on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you make a mistake.
• The color wheel will be referred to throughout the term, so keep it in a handy place. One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your
Art Book.
∞ Image Link: Example of Expanded Color Wheel
∞ Video Link: Create an Expanded Color Wheel L4 L49, L5 L1, & L6 L25

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Spend time looking outside and naming the colors that you
see. Talk about the primary colors that you would use to mix
those colors.

Color Theory: Neutral
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, color wheel, neutral paint, pencil, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Recall primary, secondary, and tertiary colors.

➜ INTRO
Today, we will learn how neutral affects color.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Adding Neutral to Watercolor L5 L3

➜ PRACTICE
• In your Art Book, lightly sketch in 24 two-inch squares. Try to place them in sets of two because you will use two squares per color. You
have 12 colors, so you will need 24 squares in total. You may need to use two or more sheets of paper. Beginning with Quinacridone Pink,
paint your first square.
• Pull a brush full of Quinacridone Pink to a clean place on your palette. Clean your brush. Dip the tip of your brush in the neutral and add it
to this pink.
• Paint that new color in the square next to the Quinacridone Pink.
• Discuss your observations.
• Do this same thing with red-orange, orange, and yellow-orange.

➜ NOTE
We will continue this during our next two Color Theory lesson periods.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe variations in color value on your nature walk and begin to apply this knowledge when working in your nature journal.

⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, color wheel, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall primary, secondary, and tertiary colors.
• Recall neutral.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

➜ PRACTICE

• Pull a brush full of Hansa Yellow to a clean place on your palette. Clean your brush. Dip the tip of your brush in the neutral and add it to
this yellow.
• Paint that new color in the square next to the Hansa Yellow.
• Discuss your observations.
• Do this same thing with yellow-green, green, and blue-green.

➜ NOTE
We will complete this during our next Color Theory lesson period.

Color Theory: Value Scales with Neutral Watercolor in the Quinacridone Pink Family
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall neutral.

➜ INTRO
Students will change the value of their colors this term using neutral. These colors will be known as “shades” of their color.

➜ NOTE
There are three lesson times to complete four value scales in the Quinacridone Pink family. Pace yourself accordingly.

➜ PRACTICE
Value scales with neutral
• Set up 12 value scales in your Art Book. Each value scale should have five 1” squares in it.
• Move some Quinacridone Pink to a clean well in your palette. Paint this in the first box on one value scale.
• Dip the tip of your brush into neutral. Be careful to add a very tiny amount each time. Paint this new shade into the next square on the
Quinacridone Pink scale.
• Add another tiny amount of neutral to the pink and paint it in the next square in the same scale.
• Do this until you have filled in your Quinacridone Pink scale.
• Repeat these steps to complete value scales for red-orange, orange, and yellow-orange.
∞ Image Link: Value Scales by Adding Neutral

➜ DISCUSS
What happened to the color as you added more neutral?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for objects on your nature walk that are different values of orange.

Color Theory: Brush Drawing of Apple, Orange, or Grapefruit
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, apple, orange, or grapefruit

➜ RECAP
• Recall tertiary colors.
• Recall complementary colors.
• Recall neutral and how to create shades of colors.
• Recall how to make swatches in order to match colors.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set an apple, orange, or grapefruit in front of you.
• Observe the piece of fruit and discuss your observations.
• Prepare your color palette and match colors by creating swatches on your paper.
• Do a brush drawing of your fruit. Add value, shading, and shadows.
∞ Image Link: Grapefruit Example

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare the colors in the brush drawing to the real piece of fruit. How accurate are your colors? How could you improve your brush drawing?

• OCCUPATIONS
Use pastels to draw a tree from memory in your Art Book. Add a neutral to your colors for the darker values.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE

• OCCUPATIONS
Shading with pastels is similar to watercolors. Use pastels to draw an animal from memory in your Art Book. Add a neutral to your colors for the darker values.

Color Theory: Pastel Home Painting
⍞ Materials: pastels, cloth, Art Book

➜ NOTE
This will take two lessons.

➜ RECAP
• Recall value.
• Recall how to create a shade in watercolor.

➜ PRACTICE
Watch the video tutorial first.
Do a pastel painting of your home.
• Sit outside, facing your home so that you can see all of it, from foundation to roof.
• Shading with pastels is similar to watercolors.
• Draw your home applying the knowledge you have gained of shades (using neutral for the darker values and white to lighten
your values).
∞ Image Link: Pastel Home Painting Example
∞ Video Link: Pastel Home Painting L5 L21

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to the object. How well did you apply shades of color?

assignment_description:
Drawing: Recalling What You Know
⍞ Materials: drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, cube-shaped objects

PREP: Students will need cube-shaped objects and rectangular prisms for still life. These are objects such as tissue boxes, books, wooden signs, etc.

➜ RECAP
Recall drawing techniques and skills learned in previous levels.
• How to set up your drawing board and Art Book.
• Measuring with the eye and drawing tool. Optional: Watch the video to refresh skill if needed.
• How to draw a cube.
• Simple shading with three values.
∞ Video Link: Measuring with the Eye and Drawing Tool
∞ Video Link: Drawing Cubes L4 L3 & L5 L25 (optional if you need a refresher)

➜ PRACTICE
• Students should set up their drawing boards on their laps with their Art Books clipped in and opened to a new page.
• Set a tissue box or other cubical object at the student’s eye level where the student can easily lift their eyes and see the entire object.
• Discuss observations.
• Have students draw the cube in the air with a finger.
• Have students draw and fill in the whole cubical form (not an outline) in their Art Book using the side of the pastel and measuring
with the eye.
• Repeat as time allows. Turn the cube so that it is at different angles and draw again.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS
Spend time setting up a still life and drawing from life.

Drawing: Shading and Light Source
⍞ Materials: drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, lamp or spotlight, cube-shaped objects

PREP: Students will need cube-shaped objects and rectangular prisms for still life. These are objects such as tissue boxes, books, wooden signs, etc.

➜ WARM UP
• Set up your drawing board and draw three or four cubes using charcoal.
• Use large shoulder movements, not finger, wrist, or elbow movements.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Shading and Lightsource L5 L26

➜ PRACTICE
Set up a still life with a specific light source. Draw what you see, focusing on shading.
∞ Video Link: Setting Up a Still Life L1 L10 & L5 L26

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS
Set a cube above eye level (worm’s eye view) and draw what you see using the eye measuring technique. Set the cube below eye level (bird’s eye view) and draw what you see.

Drawing: Still Life
⍞ Materials: drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, lamp or spotlight, cube-shaped objects

PREP: Students will need cube-shaped objects and rectangular prisms for still life. These are objects such as tissue boxes, books, wooden signs, etc.
∞ Video Link: Setting Objects at Eye Level L2 L61, L3 L52, & L5 L27 (optional if you need a refresher)

➜ WARM UP
• Set up your drawing board and draw three or four cubes using charcoal.
• Use large shoulder movements, not finger, wrist, or elbow movements.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a still life with a specific light source, just like you did in the last lesson. Draw what you see, focusing on shadows.
• Set the still life above eye level (worm’s eye view) and draw what you see using the eye measuring technique. Set the still life below eye
level (bird’s eye view) and draw what you see.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• TEACHER TIP
Remind your student to be careful to hold their pastels correctly when drawing in the nature journal.

Drawing: Simple Figures
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design and Craft-Work, charcoal, Art Book, drawing board, people in action

PREP: Read “Figure Drawing” p.194-197

➜ VIEW
Watch this video first. It is very inspirational and fun!
∞ Video Link: A Simple & Powerful Way for Sketching People
This YouTube video is also on gesture drawing. It’s a bit much for students, but it will give you an idea of how to go about the lesson.
∞ Video Link: Gesture Drawing

➜ NOTE
Students will need to observe people in action. Consider going to the park, grocery store, etc., for this lesson.

➜ RECAP
Recall proportion.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Simple figures
• Compare the stick figures to the fully formed figures.
⍞ Image Resource: "Plate 89” p.195

➜ PRACTICE
Draw stick figures in action.
• Observe people in action - running, walking, biking, etc.
• Discuss observations.
• Notice the angles of their legs and arms when walking, running, sitting, bending, etc.
• Draw what you see.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS
Choose a new location or situation and continue to practice
drawing stick figures in action. Discuss what you see.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Notice people in action by the way. Discuss your observations.

Drawing: Charcoal Value Scale
⍞ Materials: chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl or kneaded eraser

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm motion for three to four minutes.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Using Erasers As Drawing Tools L5 L31

➜ PRACTICE
Create a value scale using charcoal.
• Set up a value scale with ten value spaces in your Art Book.
• Do ten values ranging from very dark to very light. Use your kneaded or pink pearl eraser to get the values you desire.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS
Continue to practice still life drawings with charcoal in your Art
Book. Use erasers to create values.

Drawing: Still Life
⍞ Materials: chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl or kneaded eraser, cylindrical, conical, and cubical forms

PREP: Students will need cylindrical, conical, and cubical forms for setting up a still life. These are things such as flower pots, vases, tissue boxes, etc.

➜ NOTE
Refer back to Term 1 in Level 4 Art Lessons for previous charcoal value scales.

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm motion for three to four minutes.

➜ RECAP
Recall value, shading, shadow, and light source.

➜ PRACTICE
Still life drawing
• Set up a still life with simple forms such as cylinders, flower pots, tissue boxes, vases, etc.
• Add a light source.
• Draw the still life using charcoal in your Art Book. Focus on adding the values that you see. Use your erasers to add the lightest values.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

Drawing: Landscape
⍞ Materials: chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl or kneaded eraser

PREP: Students will need to go outside or look out a window or door for this lesson.

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm motion for three to four minutes.

➜ RECAP
Recall value, shadow, shading, and light source.

➜ PRACTICE
Watch the tutorial video.
∞ Video Link: Landscape Drawing with Charcoal
Draw a landscape using charcoal in your Art Book.
• Select a view with distant objects. Consider going outside or looking out a window or door.
• Focus on adding the values that you see. Use your erasers to add the lightest values.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to the view.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

Drawing: Head Proportions
⍞ Materials: chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl or kneaded eraser

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm motion for three to four minutes.

➜ RECAP
Recall proportion.

➜ PRACTICE
Head proportions
• Students will need to work along with the video tutorial in their art book.
∞ Video Tutorial: Proportions of the Human Head L5 L34

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS
Ask a family member or friend to sit for you. Do an observational drawing of their head, neck, and shoulders.

Drawing: The Human Bust
⍞ Materials: chalk, chalkboard, charcoal, drawing board, Art Book, human model

➜ NOTE
The student will need a real person as a model in this lesson and the next.

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm movements.

➜ RECAP
Recall head proportions.

➜ PRACTICE
Observational drawing of human model
• Be sure to use a real person as a model.
• Use charcoals in your Art Book.
• Use proper proportions of the human head.
• Only draw the bust of your model: head, neck, and shoulders.
∞ Image Link: Human Bust Example
∞ Video Link: How to Draw a Portrait in Charcoal from Life

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to the model.

• OCCUPATIONS
Draw more figures in action or close-ups of heads in your Art Book.

Drawing: Human Model
⍞ Materials: chalk, chalkboard, charcoal, drawing board, Art Book, human model

➜ NOTE
The student will need a real person as a model in this lesson.

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm movements.

➜ RECAP
Recall head proportions.

➜ PRACTICE
Observational drawing of human model
• Be sure to use a real person as a model. This time, you will draw the entire person from head to toe.
• Ask your model to pose standing up.
• Use charcoals in your Art Book.
• Make sure to use proper proportions of the human head.
• Don't worry if this feels weird because you have zoomed up on the bust before. Do the best you can to draw the entire person.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to the model.

Drawing: Landscape View
⍞ Materials: pastels, Art Book

PREP: Read “Sketching From Nature” Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work p.203-210.

➜ NOTE
Students will need a landscape view with objects far away, not close up.

➜ NOTE
This lesson is best done outside, but students may observe a landscape from a window or door if necessary.

➜ NOTE
We will repeat this lesson several times because the more you practice, the stronger you will become.

➜ RECAP
Recall landscape and perspective.

➜ PRACTICE
Landscape drawing
• Look at the distant landscape.
• Notice what is at eye level, below, and above.
• Notice what is close and what is far away.
• Notice what happens as things like trees, light poles, or buildings move farther away from you.
• Discuss observations.
• Do an observational drawing of your landscape in your Art Book with pastels.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS
Draw additional landscapes in different environments. Stand on a sidewalk and look down the sidewalk into the distance or stand in a long hallway and observe. Discuss what you see.

Drawing: Landscape View
⍞ Materials: pastels, Art Book, a landscape view with objects far away, not close up.

➜ NOTE
This lesson is best done outside, but students may observe a landscape from a window or door if necessary.

➜ NOTE
We will repeat this lesson several times because the more you practice, the stronger you will become.

➜ RECAP
Recall landscape and perspective.

➜ PRACTICE
Landscape drawing
• Look at the distant landscape.
• Notice what is at eye level, below, and above.
• Notice what is close and what is far away.
• Notice what happens as things like trees, light poles, or buildings move farther away from you.
• Discuss observations.
• Do an observational drawing of your landscape in your Art Book with pastels.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe the landscape around you today on your nature walk.
Discuss observations.

Drawing: Landscape from Memory
⍞ Materials: pastels, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Recall landscape and perspective.

➜ PRACTICE
Do a landscape painting from memory. Consider perspective and include objects that are up close and in the distance.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS
Fold a piece of cardstock in half to make a card. Brush draw a landscape from memory on the card. Write a note of encouragement inside and give it to someone you care about.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe the landscapes and how they change as you walk or drive by the way.

Drawing: Children at Play
⍞ Materials: pastels, Art Book, drawing board, children at play

PREP: Students will need to be in an environment where children are playing.

➜ NOTE
We will repeat this lesson several times because the more you practice, the stronger you will become.

➜ RECAP
Recall figure drawing.

➜ PRACTICE
Watch the tutorial for some inspiration (optional). Notice how the children are frozen in time.
∞ Video Link: Children Playing on the Beach at Lake Michigan
• Observe children at play.
• Discuss what you notice about the way the body moves. For example, what does it look like when you are running to kick a ball?
• Demonstrate the motion.
• Draw children playing in your Art Book.
• Share your drawings with your teacher.
∞ Image Link: Example of Children at Play 1
∞ Image Link: Example of Children at Play 2

• OCCUPATIONS
Paint children playing from memory in your Art Book.

Drawing: Children at Play
⍞ Materials: charcoals, Art Book, drawing board

PREP: Students will need to be in an environment where children are playing.

➜ RECAP
Recall figure drawing.

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe children at play.
• Discuss what you notice about the way the body moves. For example, what does it look like when you are running to kick a ball?
• Demonstrate the motion.
• Draw children playing in your Art Book.
• Share your drawings with your teacher.

Drawing: Children at Play
⍞ Materials: soft pastels, cloth, color wheel

PREP: Students will need to be in an environment where children are playing.

➜ RECAP
Recall figure drawing.

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe children at play.
• Discuss what you notice about the way the body moves. For example, what does it look like when you are riding a bike or a scooter?
• Demonstrate the motion.
• Draw children playing in your Art Book.
• Share your drawings with your teacher.

• OCCUPATIONS
Observe people all around you. Continue drawing people in action.

Drawing: Free Drawing
⍞ Materials: soft pastels, charcoal, erasers, cloth, color wheel, Art Book

➜ NOTE
There have been many new skills introduced. Students need margin to settle in with these skills and learn to apply them successfully in the artwork of their choosing. Allow your student to choose one of the subject matters covered this term and whether they would like to work in pastel or charcoal. The students will spend these lessons trying to recall and apply the skills on their own. You can ask probing questions such as "tell me about your drawing" or "what is the light source?" but do not attempt to instruct them any further.

➜ RECAP
Recall figure drawing, landscape, and still life.

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe children at play, a person sitting, a house down the street, or a still life.
• Discuss what you notice about the object(s) you would like to draw.
• Use pastels or charcoal.
• Create a drawing of what you have chosen.
• Add value and shading.
• Share your drawings with your teacher.

assignment_description:
Brush Drawing: Vertical, Horizontal, and Oblique Brushstrokes
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, Art Book

PREP: Read lesson and preview plate.
"Plate I" Brush Drawing

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use. Review brushstrokes learned previously.

➜ INTRO
Today, we are going to review basic brushstrokes learned in level 4. It is important to do this refresher at the beginning of every brush drawing term.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Beginning Brushstrokes L4 L25, L5 L49, & L6 L1
⍞ Image Resource: "Plate I" Brush Drawing

➜ PRACTICE
Vertical, horizontal, and oblique brushstrokes
• Practice all directions.
• Change colors as the student desires.
⍞ Image Resource: "Plate I" Brush Drawing

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Which brushstrokes look best?

➜ NOTE
Students should clean up at the end of each lesson. Clean, dry, and store brush. Leave palette open to dry.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for leaves of different shapes during nature walks. Encourage students to think about how a brush stroke might be used to create a certain leaf in their nature journal.

Brush Drawing: Curved Brushstrokes
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, Art Book

PREP: Read lesson and preview plate.
"Plate II” Brush Drawing

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use.

➜ INTRO
Today, we are going to review the curved brushstrokes learned in level 4. It is important to do this review at the beginning of the year as a refresher.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Curved Brushstrokes L4 L31, L5 L50, & L6 L2

➜ PRACTICE
Curved brushstrokes
• Practice all lines until students are able to do the stroke with ease.
• Change colors as student desires.
⍞ Image Resource: "Plate II" Brush Drawing

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Which brush strokes look best?

• OCCUPATIONS
Use a different color and make patterns using curved brush strokes. Or work through the alphabet using cursive letters.

Brush Drawing: Birds in a Tree
⍞ Materials: brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, Art Book, twigs, birds, and flowers to observe

PREP: Read through the lesson plan on the wikiHow website. You will teach this lesson to your students based on the wikiHow lesson. The purpose is for students to apply what they have been learning in watercolor technique. View the examples.
∞ Link: How to Paint Birds on Spring Branches in Watercolor
∞ Image Link: Example 1
∞ Image Link: Example 2
∞ Image Link: Example 3

➜ NOTE
You will have 4 lessons to finish this project.

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall curved brushstrokes.
• Recall underpainting.

➜ PRACTICE
Work through the watercolor drawing of multiple, different types of birds in a tree.
• Consider the curved lines of the birds' bodies, tree branches, etc.
• Do an underpainting to begin.
• Use lifting in your brush drawing.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Spend time observing animals during your nature walk today.
Focus on the curves that you see.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Discuss uses for this watercolor technique. Notice the sky. How
could you create the sky using a wash and lifting technique?

Brush Drawing: Shading with Watercolors
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, hair dryer, Art Book

PREP: Read lesson and preview plate, continued on the next page.
“Shaded Monochrome - Plate VI” Brush Drawing

➜ RECAP
Recall monochromatic, changing value with adding or subtracting water, and adding neutral.

➜ INTRO
This term, we will learn how to apply value and shading with watercolors.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Value changes in leaves
• Study Plate VI and discuss value changes.
⍞ Image Resource: “Shaded Monochrome - Plate VI” Brush Drawing
• Observe live leaves and discuss value changes.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Applying Value By Layering L5 L55

➜ PRACTICE
Shading with watercolor (also known as the “wet on dry” technique in watercolor)
• Sketch thumbnails in your Art Book.
• Apply a wash to the entire thumbnail. Allow it to dry. Tip: use a hair dryer on cool.
• Paint a second layer of the same color ¾ of the way across your thumbnail. Allow it to dry.
• Paint a third layer of the same color ½ of the way across your thumbnail. Allow it to dry.
• Paint a fourth layer of the same color ¼ of the way across your thumbnail. Allow it to dry.

➜ DISCUSS
What did you observe about the value change as you completed this work?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe value changes in the things that you collect for nature study. How would you apply your knowledge of layering when brush drawing in your nature journal?

Brush Drawing: Shaded Monochrome
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, leaf, hair dryer, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Recall monochromatic, value, and layering.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Value changes
“Shaded Monochrome - Plate VI” Brush Drawing

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Monochrome Leaf Watercolor Painting L5 L56

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe a leaf closely.
• How many different values do you notice in the leaf?
• Do an underpainting to begin.
• Paint what you see in the leaf using the layering technique.
• Count how many values you have when finished.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare painting to live leaf. Do they have the same value changes?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe value changes in specimens during nature study. How would you apply your knowledge of layering when brush drawing in your nature journal?

Brush Drawing: House Plant
⍞ Materials: brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, house plant, hair dryer, Art Book

➜ NOTE
This will stretch over three lessons, so try to keep the plant in the same pot.

➜ RECAP
Recall monochromatic, value, and layering.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Set up the house plant at eye level, observe, and discuss what you see.
∞ Image Link: House Plant Example

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Hanging House Plant - Pothos Watercolor Painting

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe the plant closely.
• How many different values do you notice in the leaves?
• Do an underpainting to begin.
• Paint what you see in the leaves using the layering technique.
• Count how many values you have when finished.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare painting to live leaf. Do they have the same value changes?

• OCCUPATIONS
Paint a still life in your Art Book using these techniques. Keep your painting monochromatic and only focus on changing the values.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe value changes in specimens during nature study. How would you apply your knowledge of layering when brush drawing in your nature journal?

Recall value, shading, lifting, and graduated washes.

Objects

Brush Drawing: Painting a Still Life in Watercolor
⍞ Materials: computer for watching tutorial

➜ RECAP
Recall value, shading, lifting, and graduated washes.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Painting Still-Life in Watercolor

➜ NOTE
You will watch this video during one class time. For the next three lessons, you will create a still life and paint it in your Art Book. In the video, he references art gum. We have not yet learned the skill of masking. When areas need to be white in your still lifes, remember to leave the white of the paper.

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch this video during one class time.
• Spend time gathering objects that you can use in a still life. Discuss how they are similar/different. How do they reflect light? What do
their shadows look like?

Brush Drawing: Painting a Still Life in Watercolor
⍞ Materials: brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, Art Book or watercolor paper, objects for a still life

➜ RECAP
Recall value, shading, lifting, graduated washes, and what you remember from the video tutorial.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up either one still life that can remain in your space and you can work from for three lessons, or plan to set up and paint a new one each day.
• Keep the objects simple but interesting.
• Remember all that you have learned in drawing, brush drawing, and color theory, and apply it as you work out your still life in your Art
Book or on a piece of watercolor paper.

➜ PRACTICE
Do observational brush drawings of these objects, applying all of the techniques that you have learned thus far.

➜ SELF-EVALUATION
Compare your paintings to the objects.
